telescope.open: You try to pry open the lens housing. It's rusted shut, fused by decades of neglect into a single corroded unit. Even tetanus would struggle to get in there.
telescope.push: You push the telescope. It swivels grudgingly on its mount, squealing like a cat in a bath. It now points at a slightly different section of blur. Progress.
telescope.pull: You pull the telescope toward you. The eyepiece lurches uncomfortably close to your face, offering an intimate view of absolutely nothing. The lens is still cracked. Your dignity is also cracked.

railing.talk: You address the railing sternly, reminding it of its structural responsibilities. It creaks noncommittally. Management has been notified. Management does not care.
railing.pull: You pull the railing toward you. It bends inward with a groan of rusty protest, then slowly bends back, swaying like a drunk trying to look sober. You decide to stop testing your luck.
railing.open: The railing is not a door. It's barely a railing. Asking it to be something else entirely seems unfair to everyone involved.

tower-view.take: You want to take the view. It's a view. It exists in the space between your eyes and the horizon. You can't fold it up and pocket it, though several artists have tried.
tower-view.talk: You shout into the distance. The wind carries your words away to places that don't care. An echo returns, but it sounds vaguely mocking.
tower-view.push: You lean forward to push the... view? The view remains unmoved. Figuratively and literally. You feel foolish, which the view seems to enjoy.
tower-view.pull: You reach out as if to pull the horizon closer. It stays exactly where it is, smugly distant. The horizon has always been better at this game than you.
tower-view.open: You can't open a view. It's already open. That's the entire point of views. This is elementary stuff.

mountains.talk: You call out to the mountains. They do not answer. They've been ignoring people for geological ages and they're not about to stop for you.
mountains.push: You extend your arms toward the distant peaks and push. The mountains do not move. They were here before you and will be here long after. Your arms feel silly.
mountains.pull: You mime pulling the mountains closer. They remain exactly where they are, hundreds of miles away, thoroughly unimpressed by your mime work.
mountains.open: You can't open mountains. That's not how geology works. Although, to be fair, you're in a kingdom with a petrification curse, so perhaps normal rules don't apply. They still don't open, though.

tower-ivy.talk: You compliment the ivy on its persistence. It rustles slightly in the breeze. Whether this is acknowledgment or contempt is impossible to say. Plants are inscrutable like that.
tower-ivy.push: You push the ivy against the wall. It's already against the wall. It's been against the wall for decades. You've accomplished nothing, which is becoming a theme.
tower-ivy.pull: You yank a fistful of ivy from the stonework. Mortar crumbles and a small stone tumbles free. The tower groans. You've just accelerated entropy. Congratulations.
tower-ivy.open: You part the ivy like a curtain, expecting a hidden alcove or secret passage. Behind it: wall. Just wall. The ivy was not hiding anything except its deep commitment to property damage.

telescope-lens.talk: You hold the lens up and address it. Through the magnification, your own face stares back, distorted and unflattering. The lens has nothing to say that your reflection hasn't already communicated.
telescope-lens.push: You nudge the lens across the shelf. It rolls slightly, catches the light, and projects a tiny rainbow onto the stone wall. Beautiful. Useless. Like most things here.
telescope-lens.pull: You slide the lens toward you across the dusty shelf, leaving a clean trail in the grime. A tiny archaeological record of the moment you decided to touch things.
telescope-lens.open: It's a lens. A single piece of curved glass. There is nothing to open. It's already as open as glass gets, which is transparent.
telescope-lens.take: You reach for the telescope lens. It's heavy glass, ground with precision by someone who cared about seeing things clearly.

observation-platform.pull: You pull at the platform railing. The wood splinters but holds. It was built to withstand weather, not adventurers, but it's making an effort.

responses.look: The observation platform is a generous name for what is essentially a flat stone surface with ambitions. Flagstones are cracked and uneven, with weeds pushing through the gaps. It creaks when you shift your weight, which is not the reassurance you were hoping for from the thing between you and a fatal drop.
responses.take: You consider prying up a flagstone. But removing pieces of the floor while standing on said floor ranks among the worse ideas you've had today, and the competition is fierce.
responses.use: You're standing on it. That's the primary function of a platform. You're already using it to its full potential, which admittedly isn't saying much.
responses.talk: You address the platform, thanking it for its continued structural support. A flagstone shifts slightly underfoot. You choose to interpret this as a 'you're welcome' rather than a warning.
responses.open: There's no trapdoor in the platform. You checked. Twice. It's solid stone all the way down, which is either reassuring or disappointing depending on how badly you wanted a secret passage.
responses.push: You stomp on the flagstones experimentally. They hold. One wobbles. You stop stomping. The scientific method has limits when the laboratory is at a considerable height.
responses.pull: You try to lift a flagstone. It doesn't budge. The mortar between the stones is the only thing in this tower that seems to be doing its job properly.
responses.default: You examine the platform beneath your feet. It's old, cracked, and questionably stable. Rather like the kingdom itself.

responses.look: A weathered wooden sign that once read 'DO NOT LEAN' in stern capital letters. It has itself fallen over and now lies on the platform, face-up, delivering its warning to the sky. The irony is so thick you could lean on it. But you probably shouldn't lean on anything up here.
responses.take: You pick up the sign, consider it briefly, then put it back down. Carrying around a 'DO NOT LEAN' sign feels like a cry for help you're not ready to make.
responses.use: You prop the sign back up against the railing. It immediately falls over again. The sign has made its philosophical position on standing upright quite clear.
responses.talk: You read the sign aloud: 'DO NOT LEAN.' Sound advice for the railing, the tower, the kingdom, and frankly, your life choices. The sign offers no further commentary.
responses.open: It's a sign, not a book. Although given the state of literacy in this kingdom, the distinction may be academic.
responses.push: You nudge the fallen sign with your foot. It slides across the flagstones and comes to rest at a new angle. It now reads 'DO NOT LEAN' to a slightly different audience of no one.
responses.pull: You pull the sign toward you. The wood is soft with rot and a corner breaks off in your hand. The sign now reads 'DO NOT LEA.' Still good advice, presumably.
responses.default: You contemplate the fallen warning sign. It tried to help. Nobody listened. A parable for the ages.

responses.look: The watchtower is built from grey stone blocks, each one placed with care by builders who are now almost certainly dead. Mortar crumbles between the joints, and several stones have shifted ominously outward, as though the tower is slowly exhaling. Stubbornness and ivy are doing most of the structural work at this point.
responses.take: You tug at a loose stone. It shifts, and dust trickles from above. Somewhere in the tower's architecture, a load-bearing assumption has been challenged. You push the stone back and pretend nothing happened.
responses.use: You're not sure what you'd do with a wall. Lean on it, perhaps, but the sign had opinions about leaning and frankly the sign made a good point.
responses.talk: You press your ear to the stone. It's cold and silent. If walls could talk, these ones would probably just scream.
responses.open: You search the stones for a hidden switch or secret door. You find nothing but crumbling mortar and a spider who seems annoyed by the intrusion.
responses.push: You push against the tower wall. A trickle of mortar dust falls from somewhere above. The tower was already dying slowly. You don't need to help.
responses.pull: You work a loose stone free. Behind it is more stone. And behind that, presumably, more stone. It's stone all the way down. You put it back.
responses.default: You run your hand along the ancient stonework. Cold, rough, and slowly falling apart. Rather like morale.

responses.look: The tripod supporting the telescope is a corroded brass affair, listing to one side like a sailor three drinks in. One leg is shorter than the others. It's bolted to the platform, which is the only reason the whole apparatus hasn't toppled over the edge.
responses.take: The tripod is bolted to the platform with rivets that have fused into the stone through sheer oxidation. You'd need a blacksmith and a grudge to remove it.
responses.use: You try to adjust the tripod's legs to level the telescope. One leg moves. The other two have opinions about remaining exactly where they are. The telescope is now pointed at a different patch of sky. Equally useless.
responses.talk: You address the tripod's structural inadequacy. It leans further, sulking. Motivational speaking is clearly not your calling.
responses.open: Tripods don't open. They stand. Or in this case, lean. With great commitment and questionable results.
responses.push: You push one of the tripod legs. The entire telescope assembly swivels with a reluctant screech. Brass flakes rain down like sad confetti. Nothing is improved.
responses.pull: You pull at a tripod leg. It doesn't come free, but it does bend slightly, adding a new dimension to the telescope's already creative interpretation of 'level.'
responses.default: You examine the tripod. It's doing its best, which is not very good.

responses.look: A stone shelf built into the tower wall, thick with dust and the ghosts of things once stored here. A few scratches in the dust suggest something was recently placed -- or removed. The shelf itself is solid, which makes it the most reliable piece of construction in the entire watchtower.
responses.take: It's a shelf carved into the wall. You'd need to take the wall with it, and the wall is currently employed holding up the rest of the tower. Union rules.
responses.use: You could store something on the shelf, but your inventory management system doesn't support depositing items in random locations. Bureaucracy extends even to adventuring, apparently.
responses.talk: You ask the shelf if it's holding up alright. It's a shelf. It's literally holding up. That's the whole job. You feel the question was redundant.
responses.open: It's an open shelf. It's already open. It's been open since the day some mason chiseled it out of the wall. This is as open as shelving gets.
responses.push: You push on the shelf, half-expecting a secret mechanism. Nothing happens. It's just a shelf. Not everything is a puzzle. Sometimes masonry is just masonry.
responses.pull: You grip the shelf's edge and pull. Stone doesn't flex. The shelf remains exactly as it was. Your fingertips, however, are now grey with centuries-old dust.
responses.default: You examine the dusty shelf. It's a shelf. It has dust. The narrative possibilities are limited.

responses.look: The village sprawls below the watchtower like a collection of boxes a giant child abandoned mid-tantrum. Thatched roofs, cobbled streets, and the occasional plume of chimney smoke suggest life continues down there, oblivious to the curse slowly turning the kingdom to stone. The village square is visible to the west.
responses.take: You can't take an entire village. You can barely take responsibility for your own actions. One thing at a time.
responses.use: The village is below you. To use it, you'd need to go down there. The stairs are to the west. This isn't a complicated logistics problem.
responses.talk: You shout down to the village. A dog barks in response. A door slams. The village has heard enough from people shouting from towers and would like to be left alone.
responses.open: You can't open a village. It's already there. It's been there for centuries. It opened itself, presumably, at some point in the distant past.
responses.push: You can't push a village from up here. Gravity works in one direction, and even if it didn't, the village didn't do anything to deserve being pushed.
responses.pull: You can't pull the village closer. It's exactly as far away as the bottom of the tower, which is as close as it can get without significant urban planning.
responses.default: You gaze down at the village. It looks small from up here. Everything does, except your problems.

responses.look: A vast forest blankets the landscape beyond the village, its canopy a dark green sea stretching to the horizon. Something about the forest's colour seems wrong in patches -- grey creeping through the green like mold through bread. The petrification curse doesn't discriminate between stone and wood, apparently.
responses.take: You want to take the forest. The entire forest. You'd need pockets the size of a kingdom, and even then the squirrels would object.
responses.use: The forest is too far away to use from here. You'd need to climb down and walk there, like a person who understands how distance works.
responses.talk: You address the forest from the watchtower. The trees rustle. Probably the wind. Hopefully the wind. Trees that respond to conversation would be an entirely new category of problem.
responses.open: You can't open a forest. You can enter one, sure, but that requires walking there first. The east path leads toward the forest bridge, if you've found enough reason to go.
responses.push: Pushing a forest from the top of a watchtower. This is the caliber of problem-solving that got the kingdom cursed in the first place, presumably.
responses.pull: You reach toward the distant forest. It does not come to you. Forests are famously sedentary. You'll have to go to it.
responses.default: You study the distant forest from the tower. It looks peaceful. Forests always do, right up until they aren't.

responses.look: You squint into the distance. There's a grey smudge on the horizon that might be a castle, might be a mountain, might be a weather system with delusions of architecture. Without a working telescope, it's impossible to tell.
responses.take: You can't take a castle. Especially not one that's several days' journey away and potentially cursed. Add it to the long list of things you can't have.
responses.use: The castle -- if that's what it is -- is far too distant to use from here. You'd need to actually travel there, which requires the kind of commitment and poor judgment you're still working up to.
responses.talk: You shout toward the distant grey shape. Your voice carries about forty feet before the wind shreds it to nothing. The castle does not respond. It has bigger problems than you.
responses.open: You can't open a castle from the top of a watchtower. That's not how castles work. That's not how anything works.
responses.push: The castle is several hundred miles away. You push the air in its general direction. The air is uncooperative. The castle is unaware.
responses.pull: You can't pull a castle across a kingdom. Even the curse isn't that ambitious.
responses.default: You stare at the distant grey shape on the horizon. It stares back, or seems to. Probably just the light.
